speaker: interviewee
text: We already finished it. Not too much to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll four with Leopold Page. Roll four, Leopold Page. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The period in Brinnlitz from November 1944 till May 8, at the night, 12 o'clock at night, 1945, was extremely interesting part of our life and our history over there. We've heard, everybody of us, that how long Schindler is with us, will nothing happen to us. The minute he was not absent, we were very uneasy until, as a point I give you a situation what him came in in the first weeks in January 1945. Two cattle train with about 136 people from Golleschau-- this was a sub-camp-- sub-camp of Auschwitz came. They were six nights, seven days locked up without the food, without opening the gate, and every camp refused to accept them. They came early morning on Sunday to the Brinnlitz and Schindler was not there. Mrs. Schindler, who deserves a tremendous credit for saving the lives of those people, that meant that cattle train should be open, and the people should be taken out because factory need much more worker because we're producing extremely important artillery shell for the army, and we have to deliver this. We never produce anything. Everything was faked. And anything he needed to deliver he was buying on the black market, Mr. Schindler, from another manufacturer, who were making the same shell. Our shells were so bad that the ones we delivered, they start investigating why-- why they are so bad. But Schindler was not there. And the Leipold, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sturmfuhrer Leipold, who was in charge of the SS, about 40 of them, but they were elderly. The young, already, they were sent to the front. They were, like, in 40s and 50s, didn't want to keep this. And he want to send them back to some other camp. She told him, when you will not let these people out till Mr. Schindler will come over. We need the people. You will go to Eastern Front. You know that Mr. Schindler has a connection in Berlin. So don't interfere. He let her wait till Schindler come in. And Schindler come in and called the world as Mr. Mandela and me-- there were two-- to come over because you can open the door. You have to move the snow. And you have to cut the hinges. We cut the hinges and we took the door. From this 136, 16 was dead. And the rest, another five hours, four hours, two hours, they all will be dead. They were frozen. They were-- some of those lost ears, part ears, toes, part of nose. Someway, somehow, Mrs. Schindler and Oskar Schindler put upon themselves to play angels. And they doed everything possible to save those people's lives. They never work. He has to pay 600 marks for each of us. So even for those people who didn't work, he'd pay for them. She found medication, what was necessary, on the black market in-- in Sudetenland. And they saved this life of about 115, 120 people. No, 16 was dead so-- and then couple more die later on, but over 100 Golleschau people survived. And they survived the war, and they brought to life. And the most dramatic item was this-- that he got the order to burn the body in the oven, over there in the factory [NON-ENGLISH], the big oven. And Schindler refused. He said, in my factory, you don't burn body. We bury them. And with a religious ceremony, with Rabbi Levitoff, we buried those people with 10 people going and saying the-- the religious Kaddish. And they are buried until today. They are buried over there next to Catholic cemetery. We got a piece of land from the church because they didn't want them to be buried on Catholic cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are the condition in Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the condition was, I would say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was is a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a camp surrounded with barbed wire and everything, with German guards, with towers and everything like any other camp, but they have no right to come to the factory. He didn't allow them to do anything. But he bribed these guards. They are giving them and vodka, and money, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the conditions, the living conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living conditions were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, better than any other camp. But remember that everything was bought on the black market. Sometimes the food was not enough. But one day in Brno, the factory was bombarded by Russians, and the German were withdrawing from Brno. He went there because he knew a cigarette factory over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went, loaded with a truck with some friends from [INAUDIBLE], brought the cigarettes back to us, and, on the way, they were stopped by patrol, German patrol, and they ask you what you have? He said cigarettes for mein Juden. So he give everybody a pack of cigarettes. And they want more. He said no more. Mein Juden [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. My Jews smoking, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start to getting cigarettes for which we could get from Czech side some bread or something else. People can exchange it. And he tried very hard. But on the end of the rainbow, he used all his money. He, in 1944, Oskar Schindler and Emily Schindler were multimillionaires. In November in '44, they could take the money, like mothers and other people did, you understand, go to Vienna, to any place in the world, survive the war, and be multimillionaire. But he promise the-- the Schindler people, the Schindler Jews, the Schindler children, how he called us, that he will say with us till the five minutes after 12, and he kept the promise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, you knew that the Russian army is coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that the Russian is army coming, and the last night we decide-- we disarm, you know, we disarm the-- the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: The soldiers, the SS, we put them in a tower. I don't remember how many. It was over 30-some of them. Some maybe run away. I don't know. And we took over on the towers. We put our overalls. Instead of the stripe, we put the army overalls on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our-- I was having a guard on the main door. And we put the sign typhus on it. And I have a pistol from the-- the light bulb was sold by Oskar Schindler by this special trick to the Eastern front, you know. So his second. his name is Mossek. He was only a sergeant, like over Scharfuhrer, you know? I disarmed him with my finger. [LAUGHS] At night I sneak on him and said [INAUDIBLE], And I took his gun. And then what's happened? Schindler just left?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were staying-- We decided not to wait for the Russian to come over to liberate him. We decide to send Schindler with the group of my-- some of them my students-- to the American zone towards west. And those groups took our cars, and took Schindler, and the Germans, some engineer, and his wife, and-- and a group of six or seven people went with them. And by few days, they surrendered to American Army, We didn't know what happened to them, later on, after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's happened with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there. We took over the guards. I was one of the people participating. There was 15 of us. You understand? And we were watching. We, already, were armed, you see? One night I remember, on-- on the night-- we were interesting situation. We were in the center of-- of Brinnlitz. Around the Brinnlitz, in [INAUDIBLE], in a-- in a-- region about three, four kilometers was about 100,000 Germans soldiers, army, squeezed between east, the Russian, west, the American, Frenchmen, and we were a little point in the center. So we put a big sign, typhus, that nobody should enter. But one night, a group of SS, the black shirt SS, came over. They were demanding gasoline. And one of my students, who live in Tannenbaum, and he's living, living also. He say, Pol, give me a gun. I shoot one. I said you don't shooting anybody. We give them gasoline and tell them we are not letting the people in because the typhus is inside, and this is a labor camp. So we give him gasoline, and they left. The whole division of the black-- black SS was they were starting shooting inside. And one of the guy was wounded. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there till about on the 11th, three days later. The first Russian major, with another soldier, came on the horse, only two of them. We're liberating you. And we were laughing because we were liberated already three days before. But we asked for food. They give us our food, and then they want to make a kangaroo court on those people. And the sentence was of the kangaroo court to execute those SS people. And we stood up against them, say, we are not executing anybody. We are not a killer. And this is not a court of law. This is a kangaroo court. You take them out. We don't want to know about this. Do as what you want. We will not executed anything. But our people hanged one couple, a German couple, who was, in Dachau, a son of a bitch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? He came from Dachau to Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sending some people-- the German criminals-- criminals, you know, to be a kapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how you-- how you received your first hour of liberty when you went out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was very bad. I was sick. I was so sick that I don't know how I survive. I got-- I got, I don't know what, a touch of typhus or yellow jaundice. I was extremely sick. If it were not for Mila I would not survive. And another things, my director of my my gymnasium in Krakow, Dr. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Hirschfeld. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He stay. He's MD. There were lots of sick people still in the-- he stay in the camp. He didn't go away to support, and he helped me to get out with this. I stay about two months, three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brinnlitz, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how you came now from Brinnlitz? Is a long way from Brinnlitz to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- oh, brother. We decide-- you see, in this time, we hear the rumors, and some people came back from Poland that there are pogroms in Poland. The pogroms-- why were they started? What happened in it? We don't know exactly. But we know one thing, that the Jews who came over and demand their houses or apartments to give them back, maybe stayed in pogroms. I don't know exactly what happened. But it was in city of Krakow, city in Radom, which is not far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I even said to Mila-- we knew, already, in this time, that the family don't exist. I knew it. That they cannot-- and when they survive, we will find out each other for sure. No question about it. You see? So I decided to go to Israel. We decided to go, this was in-- in June-- May, June. I was about-- still not feeling well. And we decided to go any ways possible Mostly peasants are supposed to allowed only walking or grab a train, or some borrow or some, you know, villager give us a ride. We came to Bratislava. In Bratislava, we decide to get to Hungary. So I decided to go first on my own to Hungary, to Budapest, to find out and I left Mila in-- in the thing because was danger to ride on the train, particular for women, because the Russian soldier was killing, raping, murdering, beating up people, you understand what I mean? They were wild animals in this time, particularly in this period of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Budapest, and I found my friend, a very close friend of mine, who lives here in Los Angeles, too. And he said, Pol, go back and bring Mila here because I don't you to stay in Bratislava because you never know what happened. Hungary is still much better.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could-- when I saw the food over there, you know, and the store full of fruits, beautiful, and things like this, I said, OK. I went back, and I always carry a little short bayonet for small-- you never know in these days, you know? So I took her with me and we didn't-- on the train. And there was raping-- raping, killing, murdering, throwing people through the window by the Russian. So I stay on the platform, put her in the corner, put a blanket over her, and myself with the bayonet in my hand. We arrive in Budapest overnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then friend was waiting. And we stayed there till about September. Then we decided, we found out that the Russian demand that everybody's going back to their country. We didn't have anything to go to the country for.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, let's go back and see what we can do, maybe in Czechoslovakia. We came to Czechoslovakia. We stay a month in Prague. Then we went to [INAUDIBLE], Carlsbad and we stay in there because it was a beautiful resort, and was beautiful fall, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we found out that the Russian give another order that everybody has to register themselves and go back to their country. So we went to register as a Czechoslovakian. So they ask me where what I was born. So I was skiing in the border of Hungary and Czechoslovakia in Eastern part of Carpathian-- Uzok a small town on the-- on the-- on the border in the mountains, that I was skiing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the guy is asking me where you was born. I said in Uzok. You sure you're born in Uzok? You know, I don't speak Czech. I speak Polish, trying right to convey to him as much I could in Czechoslovakian, you see? I said-- you sure? I said, what do you mean sure? When I tell you I'm born in Uzok, I'm born in Uzok. How you got in the Uzok? You familiar with that? What do you mean I am familiar? I was skiing there. Sure I'm familiar.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're going with a train on the cliff of the mountain to get through the mountains on the south side of the mountains, and we come to Uzok. This way, they took us back to the camp. He said, oh, then you are from Uzok because I am from Uzok too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And give us permission to stay but we didn't decide with my friends that I met in Budapest, and we were travelling together to go to Germany, to east-- from east to west Germany. you see? And we were starting to do in November. And we go on that, walking in through the mountains, in the deep snow, and we arrive beginning of December to-- across the West Germany borders, and we came a few days later to Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we saw our friends, Rosner and Schindler is there and everybody is there. So we live together. And I stay over there. But I am not a person what I will run around doing nothing. I decide to do something, so I report to the UNRRA, United Nations Relief Fund. And I told them what was my occupation and everything. And I became education center director of UNRRA with [INAUDIBLE] in Munich, with a job to organize and get children to schools. Nobody could organize. I brought 1,700 children to school, promising in their papers that I will give them clothes, food, shelter, and education. And the parents, if they were not orphans, the parents can go and arrange a transfer to other countries or to emigrate to America, or to South America, to-- back to their own country. And I have, since 1945, in December, till April 1947, 1,700 children. I'm giving them diploma, graduating from high school, graduating from public school. Originally, with United Nations Relief Fund. I have the documentation here--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when then-- how you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working for UNRRA. And I applied to America. They asked me, do I have any cousin or family. I said, I have plenty family. My father was in America. The family of Pfefferberg and family of Goldfluss. There are plenty of them there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the highest officer was asking me, do you have the address? I said, my God, I don't have any address. I don't even remember where they live. I know they are in New York because my father was, for seven years, in New York. So she brought Manhattan book. But they never lived in Manhattan. They probably lived in Brooklyn. But she brought it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we open. And I see that Moses Goldfluss, Rebecca Goldfluss, about four names Goldfluss. I said positively, that's my family. Similar names in my family were there in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they wrote a letter to this. The Pfefferberg was only one. And very soon, they came over and said, no one from the Goldfluss say that you are family. So I said, well, but I want to go to America. And only the Pfefferberg. mentioned that he may be related to you. I said, see, I have family there. but they don't want to give money, so we don't know how you can get.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in between President Truman, they received 200,000 permits for displaced person. And because I was working for the United States government, for the UNRRA, and the major of US Army who was in charge of the whole [INAUDIBLE], when I have the 1,700 people, and he likes me very much, and I was teaching him skiing in the Alps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, your birthday is on March 20, in '47? Yes. You will get a visa at this day. And on the day of my birthday, he gave me a present visa for Mila and me. And April 17, or 10, or 17, we went to Hamburg. I have the $10, and Mila has $10 in the pocket. The only money we have.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was making $200 a month from UNRRA, but not the American dollars. Only the-- I could buy a pack of cigarettes for this $200. We were supposed to get paid in American dollars, but somebody was stealing the dollars, replacing with the-- the-- the-- the, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Marks.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the-- thank you. So when I came over on the boat, the captain, and the doctor, and one officer need four guys to play bridge. So they found me. I played bridge with them for the five nights that we were there, and I landed with $105 in Hamburg. That was my fortune. And then we stay in New York. I open a business with the same friend, what we came from Budapest and things. He was a year ahead in America than me because he has a brother. And he was working for an importer of leather goods from Argentina.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told him that his cousin is coming who is a specialist in this field. I never knew it, how to open the handbag. But when I was introduced to him, and he said to me, you know how to repair bags? Look, I was mechanics. I was a welder. I said, I can repair anything. So I start-- they hire me, too, and I start working. And we decide to open a repair shop. Because he was sending repairs to another place. And the guy charged a lot of money. So we said, we'll do this for half price of the other guy. And we start repair to the import of the bags what he was bringing in. Then, look, I went to the library in New York. He need to glaze something, or dye something. I pick up the book in German. I read this. I went to the store. I bought the-- I didn't even know how you call it in English, the tools, when I was buying. I draw them the pictures what I needed because I didn't speak English. I-- it worked, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we study. And we were working. And we make some money. Then he got sick. And the doctor advised him to go to California. And so he bombarded me from California everyday-- come and come, and Freddy was born. And I hate New York. I hated--

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy is your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was born on March 20, 1950, on my birthday, five minutes after 12:00 at noon. Mila didn't have the money to buy me a present. She gave me a son, and it cost me $500 extra. And Freddy was born. Beautiful little boy, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decide to go. I couldn't stand summer in New York. I couldn't stand winter in New York. Because we were living in Long Island, and I have to travel with the subway to Manhattan, you know, and it was a horrible things, you know? And the amount of money we make, it was sufficient to live-- not enough to die. You see? But then, we were developing more reputation. And we start to making. Then I bought my partner who went to-- to Los Angeles. And I took another partner. Then, a year later, in 1950, we decide to come over to California. Because he was telling me, Pol, you are a skier. Do you ski in New York? I said no. I shovel the snow, only in New York in winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I do it like this. I go in the morning to ski in the San Bernardino mountains. And afternoon, I'm going to Pacific Ocean and we swim-- swim with my little daughter. He had daughter at the same time what Freddy was born, a month later his daughter was born. And we will have a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And work, I have already a store. You will work with me, and we will have everything fine. So I said to Mila, what do we have to lose? I sold my part of-- of my business to my friend, who was partner, for $1,500. And I was consider myself I was a very rich man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I come over. I didn't want to work anymore for anybody. I opened my little shop in Beverley Hills to grow to a very big substantial business. I make quite a bit of money-- money. Good living, and I could save it. I bought a house. I have to pay mortgages. I have to pay taxes. I have everything. Then I decide-- and I always talked about the war. I consider the reason that I and Mila survive is a purpose. Other way, is no sense that why we survive and the other perish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your-- if you must leave a message to the next coming generations, and to your grandchildren, what you will say about all this what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, in the darkest moment of your life, you must think positively that you will overcome this. This is one important. The minute when you decide that you are already without any exit in the darkest situation, then you are doomed to perish. You have to have a hope that you will survive and you will overcome. This is one of the most important. Your mental attitude, spiritual attitude, the strength is in yourself to survive-- then you survive. The second thing-- remember that tragedy, what happened, happened on account-- on account of prejudice and hatred. And this is the worst killer. Love is building, and hatred is killing. And this what you have to remember. Love everybody around you with whom you work or cooperate because this will come back the same way like you're sending to the other people. And well, showing that you respect and love somebody else, the person will show you the same respect and love, and you can create a lot of friendship. And this is proven point in my life. I-- you know, you were with me in many places. Around the world, many places you will find, I have somebody who I love, and somebody who loves me. And this is what it is. Your attitude toward the people, and positive-- positive thinking, create a strength inside of you what give you a chance to overcome even the most difficult situations in their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have also a-- a daughter, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a daughter, too. Marie was born six years later, in 1956, and only two days shorter from my sister's birthday. My sister was born on February-- February 9. Marie was born on February 7. And we call-- give her a name after Mila's mother, Marie, and after second name as a Pauline, after my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Poli, one question what everybody's asking you-- What's happened then with Schindler? What was your connection when you came all this time in New York, in Los Angeles. We know, everybody know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I just start to tell you something-- that I consider that the reason why did we survive, those survivors? There are two reasons. First, off, we are the witnesses to the truth what happened. There's-- nobody can challenge what we are talking because we were there. The second, our obligation is to talk about this as long as we live, to tell the world that inhumanity man to man is the biggest crime ever committed. Humanity man to man is show compassion, love, and respect to another human being. And we have to talk about this. And because I live thanks to the humanity of a man who was a German Catholic, bon vivant, womanizer, part of an espionage organization who gambled [NON-ENGLISH] with his life, and his money, and his wife's life, and he succeed-- proved the point that humanity still exists. And because exist, we have to develop and teach the young people how to love and respect. Instead, what is much easier because a child is born with a love to mother, and to family, and to-- to surrounding condition. Hatred is accumulated through a connection with people who brainwashed them, or implemented negative aspect towards other people. And this is what I decide to do. The minute I was liberated, the minute I came to-- to Munich, the minute I came to New York, the minute I came to Los Angeles, never stop talking about this. Many times I had, when I came to Jewish federation that we want to organize survivor organization, and we would like to make a Yom Hashoah in 1953. They said, forget it. You are in America. You will have to remember, you are starting brand new. I said, when we forget it, we are all doomed to perish again. We can never forget it. We have to teach people and educate the people that this, what happened, was a crime, not only against the Jew, or Poles, or gypsies, or anybody else they murder, 11 million people-- it's a crime committed by the whole world because the world didn't stand up to stomp it out and stop Hitler to doing what he did. And today, they're committing the same crime. For two and a half years, they're futzing around about Yugoslavia, when the whole world should go there and stop it. You see, and this is what I consider the lesson and obligation what the survivors have to teach these people of more compassion, more love, and more respect. When we will succeed-- I am-- I'm sorry to say that it's difficult task. It's a difficult task because survivor didn't open right away. There were only few individual cases who start to talk about this since the liberation. Now, after the interesting movie of and so many books what came on the market, what telling the truth, now the people start to understand the importance of this particular education of this horrible period of Hitler, and Stalin in-- in the-- in the-- in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
